overall_assessment: Option C is the best target contract. Current Option A is acceptable for current promotion as a bounded tactical contract, provided it is not treated as the final event-summary read model.
recommended_path: Promote current Option A as non-blocking tactical behavior for targeted sent-status hydration and push reconciliation. Establish Option C as vNext: paginated inviteables own composer row actionability, an occurrence summary contract owns exact counters and preview, and /invites/sent-statuses remains narrow for targeted recipient refresh.

findings.title: Event summary can overfetch up to 200 sent-invite rows
findings.rationale: Option A returns summary plus up to 200 items and event detail can call occurrence-level status hydration without a recipient filter. Summary/footer UI may load materially more row data than needed for counters plus a small avatar preview, but the package does not establish an unbounded runtime risk.
findings.suggested_action: Move event detail/footer to an occurrence summary/preview contract in vNext.

findings.title: Option A summary may be truncated when more than 200 sent invites exist
findings.rationale: The package states unfiltered summary can be truncated and not represent the full occurrence when more than 200 sent invites exist. If current promotion promises exact event-level counters, this becomes a correctness blocker; otherwise it is vNext debt.
findings.suggested_action: Define exact counters in the vNext occurrence summary contract and avoid using Option A as the authoritative summary source.

findings.title: Composer row actionability is better owned by paginated inviteables
findings.rationale: /contacts/inviteables is the canonical composer source and Option B/C would return sent_invite_status with each paginated inviteable row. The current two-read composer flow is acceptable when hydration is filtered, but is not the cleanest target contract.
findings.suggested_action: Adopt Option C vNext and enrich paginated inviteables with occurrence-scoped row actionability.
